def run_monte_carlo(ask_mult=1.0, bid_mult=1.0, jumps=False, gamma_val=0.1):
    results = {"inventory": {"pnl": [], "final_q": []}, 
               "symmetric": {"pnl": [], "final_q": []}}
    
    sample_paths = {}
    
    for strat in ["inventory", "symmetric"]:
        for i in range(simulations):
            t, S, q, X, PnL, r_t, p_a_t, p_b_t = simulate_market_maker(
                strategy=strat, ask_multiplier=ask_mult, bid_multiplier=bid_mult, jump_diffusion=jumps, gamma=gamma_val
            )
            results[strat]["pnl"].append(PnL[-1])
            results[strat]["final_q"].append(q[-1])
            
            if i == 0:
                sample_paths[strat] = (t, S, q, PnL, r_t, p_a_t, p_b_t)
                
    # Calculate Risk Metrics
    metrics = []
    for strat in ["inventory", "symmetric"]:
        pnl_array = np.array(results[strat]["pnl"])
        q_array = np.array(results[strat]["final_q"])
        
        metrics.append({
            "Strategy": strat.capitalize(),
            "Mean Profit": np.mean(pnl_array),
            "Std Dev (Profit)": np.std(pnl_array),
            "Mean Final q": np.mean(q_array),
            "Std Dev (Final q)": np.std(q_array),
            "Sharpe Ratio": np.mean(pnl_array) / np.std(pnl_array) if np.std(pnl_array) > 0 else 0
        })
        
    df_metrics = pd.DataFrame(metrics)
    
    # Style, format to 3 decimals, and center the HTML table
    styled_df = df_metrics.style.format(precision=3) \
        .set_table_attributes('style="margin-left: auto; margin-right: auto; width: 80%;"') \
        .set_properties(**{'text-align': 'center'}) \
        .hide(axis='index')
    
    display(HTML("<h3 style='text-align: center;'>Performance Metrics</h3>"))
    display(HTML(styled_df.to_html()))
    
    # --- Visualizations (Plotly 6-Panel Layout) ---
    t, S, q, PnL, r_t, p_a_t, p_b_t = sample_paths["inventory"]
    
    fig = make_subplots(
        rows=3, cols=2, 
        subplot_titles=(
            "Price Tracking & Optimal Quotes", 
            "Inventory Trajectory", 
            "P&L Trajectory", 
            "Trade Execution Map", 
            f"P&L Distribution (\u03B3 = {gamma_val})", 
            "Final Inventory Distribution"
        )
    )

    # Clean Hover Templates (Name in Bold, 3 decimal places, no side-box)
    hover_price = '<b>%{data.name}</b><br>Time: %{x:.3f}<br>Price: %{y:.3f}<extra></extra>'
    hover_inv   = '<b>%{data.name}</b><br>Time: %{x:.3f}<br>Inventory: %{y:.3f}<extra></extra>'
    hover_pnl   = '<b>%{data.name}</b><br>Time: %{x:.3f}<br>P&L: $%{y:.3f}<extra></extra>'
    hover_dist  = '<b>%{data.name}</b><br>P&L: %{x:.3f}<br>Count: %{y}<extra></extra>'
    hover_distq = '<b>%{data.name}</b><br>Final Q: %{x:.3f}<br>Count: %{y}<extra></extra>'

    # 1. Price Tracking (Row 1, Col 1)
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=t, y=S, mode='lines', name='Mid-market price', line=dict(color='black', width=1.5), hovertemplate=hover_price, legendgroup="price"), row=1, col=1)
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=t, y=r_t, mode='lines', name='Indifference Price', line=dict(color='lime', width=1.5), hovertemplate=hover_price, legendgroup="price"), row=1, col=1)

title: Gap Challenge Solver
text:
The Challenge
The “Gap” puzzle is a logic game where players must fill a missing cell in a grid, ensuring no symbol repeats in any row or column. My goal was to build a tool that could solve this instantly from a simple screenshot.






4x4 Grid







5x5 Grid






Gap Challenge - Each shape can only appear in a row or column once. The player must use logic by elimination to find the shape that belongs in the question mark.



While the logic puzzle itself is a standard Constraint Satisfaction Problem, digitizing the grid from a raw image proved to be the real engineering challenge. This post documents the three major iterations (“Acts”) of my Computer Vision pipeline and the lessons learned from each:

Act 1: Naive Template Matching

Act 2: Geometric Determination of Shapes

Act 3: Image Classification with SVM


Major Problem Faced with Image Recognition
A major issue was the different forms that the shapes could take based on different Gap Challenge applications. Some shapes were hollow, some backgrounds were black and some bounding grids were just lines.






Normal Version







Line Version







Dark-mode Version






Note the difference in shapes and grid colors. The hard part was building a robust solution that would be able to handle all these different versions.

title: Gap Challenge Solver
section: Act I: The Naive Approach (Template Matching)
text:
Act I: The Naive Approach (Template Matching)
My first instinct was to use Template Matching. I cropped reference images of each shape (Star, Circle, Square) and used cv2.matchTemplate to slide them over the screenshot, looking for pixel-perfect matches. I tried to account for the different size of the screenshots as well, scaling the reference shapes to match the screenshots.



Reference Images used for template matching





Heatmap matching of the reference images. Green boxes show a >90% match score against the reference image.



The Cause of Failure
This method worked well on the base example but failed when tested on other forms of the puzzle. Although I implemented multi-scale matching to handle zoom levels, the approach hit a hard ceiling when the puzzle’s visual style changed.
When the game switched to “Dark Mode” or used “Hollow” shapes, my solid-colored templates failed immediately. To fix this, I would have needed to crop and save a new template for every possible theme variation.






Failure Case 1: Hollow Shapes







Failure Case 2: Dark Mode






Lesson: Hard-coding pixel comparisons doesn’t work if the inputs vary significantly. I needed a more robust method and decided to try and use the geometric differences in shapes to differentiate them.

title: Gap Challenge Solver
text:
Act II: The Geometric Pivot (Heuristics)
For the second iteration, I moved to Feature Extraction. Instead of matching images, I used cv2.findContours to extract the shapes and analyze their geometric properties.
I built a Decision Tree based on metrics like:
- Vertices: cv2.approxPolyDP (e.g., 3 vertices = Triangle).
- Solidity: Area divided by Convex Hull Area (distinguishes solid Squares from hollow Crosses).
- Circularity: \(4\pi \times \frac{Area}{Perimeter^2}\) (distinguishes Circles from Triangles).

The “Live Scanner” Debugger
To refine my thresholds, I built a visualization tool that calculated these metrics for every cell so that I could debug the errors efficiently.



The Logic Dashboard. Watching the metrics update in real-time allowed me to tune the decision tree.




The Bottleneck
While robust against scale, this method suffered from “Whack-a-Mole” logic, where more issues would pop-up after fixing one and I realised I was trying to fix symptoms instead of fixing the root cause of the recognition issues:
- The “Hollow” Edge Case: A hollow square has low solidity, confusing the algorithm.
- The “Question Mark” Problem: The question mark consists of two separate blobs (hook + dot), breaking the single-contour logic.






Failure Case: Hollow Shapes







Failure Case: Dark Mode





The “Whack-a-Mole” logic resulted in decision trees like:
# Attempts to handle edge cases manually
if vertices > 8 and circularity < 0.6 and num_blobs == 1:
    return "Star"
elif num_blobs == 2:
    return "Question"
This resulted in more and more rules that I wasn’t even sure of and I decided a needed a system to learn these rules itself.

title: Gap Challenge Solver
section: Act III: The Robust Solution (Support Vector Machines)
text:
Act III: The Robust Solution (Support Vector Machines)
In the final phase, I treated this as a classification problem. I chose a Support Vector Machine (SVM) because they perform exceptionally well on high-dimensional binary data (pixel maps) with smaller datasets.

1. The Data Pipeline (Solving Distribution Shift)
A mistake that I made at the start was training on “perfect” data but testing on “messy” data.
I manually took multiple screenshots of the grids, with minor variations, to create a large train dataset for the SVM.
However, I realised that the SVM was not performing well, because the cropping method used for the test data was different from the perfectly cropped screenshots i was taking for the train data.
To fix this, I used the test cropping algorithm to build an automated pipeline (collect_feedback_data.py) that extracted thousands of training examples directly from screenshots.
This ensured my training data included all the real-world noise (grid lines, anti-aliasing) that the model would see in production. I utilized Otsu’s Binarization here to automatically find the optimal threshold for separating shapes from the background, solving the “Dark Mode” issue.



Automatic cropping and extraction of train data and grids for the SVM to train on.




2. The “Universal Translator” (Preprocessing)
To make the model immune to lighting changes and user cropping errors, I engineered a standardization pipeline: 1. Resize to 64x64.
2. Corner Difference Strategy: Calculate the average background color from the image corners and subtract it. This isolates the shape regardless of whether the theme is Dark or Light.
3. Centering: Find the bounding box of the shape and center it on a black canvas.


3. Fine-Tuning the Brain (Grid Search)
An SVM is only as good as its hyperparameters. Default settings often lead to overfitting, where the model memorizes the training data but fails on new inputs.
To solve this, I implemented a Grid Search to exhaustively test combinations of C (Regularization) and Gamma (Kernel Coefficient). This allowed me to mathematically find the configuration that ignored minor pixel noise while maintaining a distinct decision boundary between similar shapes (like the Circle and the hollow Ring).


4. X-Ray Vision (Verification)
Machine Learning models can be “black boxes.” To trust the system, I built a side-by-side debugger comparing the Human View (RGB) against the Machine View (Preprocessed). This allowed me to catch preprocessing errors—like the dot of the ‘?’ being filtered out as noise—before retraining the model.



SVM Debugger. Left is the game input; Right is what the model actually sees.

title: Gap Challenge Solver
text:
The Solver Logic (Backtracking)
With a reliable Vision pipeline returning a 2D matrix (e.g., [['Star', 'Empty'], ['Circle', 'Square']]), the problem reduced to a standard algorithm.
I implemented a Recursive Backtracking solver. It works by:
1. Scanning for an empty cell.
2. Guessing a shape from the available options.
3. Validating row/column constraints.
4. Recursively attempting to solve the rest of the grid.
5. Backtracking if a guess leads to a contradiction.
def solve_with_backtracking(board, universe):
    # Base Case: No empty spots left
    if not find_empty(board): return True 
    
    row, col = find_empty(board)
    
    for shape in universe:
        if is_valid_move(board, shape, row, col):
            board[row][col] = shape
            # Recursive Step
            if solve_with_backtracking(board, universe): return True
            # Backtrack
            board[row][col] = 'blank'
            
    return False

title: Gap Challenge Solver
text:
Conclusion & Tech Stack
This project was a lesson in selecting the right tool for the job. While deep learning (CNNs) could have solved this, it would have been overkill for a 5-class problem. An SVM combined with robust Data Engineering provided a lightweight, instant, and highly accurate solution (99% accuracy on test set).

Language: Python 3.10
Vision: OpenCV (Canny, Otsu’s Binarization, Contours)
ML: Scikit-Learn (SVM, GridSearch)
Frontend: Streamlit

title: Iran War Impact Analysis: Regime Shifts & Market Volatility
text:
Part 0: Rationale for this Project
So the idea for this project came as part of the interview process for a company, where i was tasked to identify large cap stocks from the Russell 1000 (market cap >USD 30 Billion) that have outperformed the S&P500.
This analysis focused on comparing the outperformance for two phases of 2026, 1. Prewar - 31th December 2025 to 27th February 2026 2. Post-war 27th February 2026 to 10th April 2026. And the tasking was to draw out some conclusions and observations from these Russell 1000 stocks that outperformed the S&P500, and see if there were any insights that I could draw.
My intuition was that it would mostly be energy stocks that outperformed, and perhaps we could extract additional insights from that point.
I did quite abit of data cleaning and data visualisation for this exercise, so i figured that i turn it into a project with risk attribution analysis for the first part, exploring the data and how the sectors and the companies did in each “market regime” (if any), and take it as a practice in data visualisation.
The idea was that the Iran War was an exogenous macro event and we could use it as a stress-test for large-cap equities.
And to expand the project a little, i figured it would be a good place to practice some quant models with Regime Detection and some Factor Modelling which would fit well with this project given its dual time period analysis.
Therefore in the second portion of this article, I explored abit of everything, going for breadth as part of an exploration into quant models and how they work, and plan to create more projects that delve into each of these models a little more in-depth in the future.
I do this in Part 2, where i try to do more complex analysis and bring in data from other sources like WRDS for analysis.

title: Iran War Impact Analysis: Regime Shifts & Market Volatility
text:
Part 1: Market Microstructure & Risk Attribution
Deconstructing the Benchmark during the Q1 2026 Geopolitical Shock.
For this portion, i got the data from Yfinance instead of for WRDS because I was under time-pressure, and got the constituents from the iShares proxy of the Russell 1000, which i figured would at least contain all the major players.
Instead of simply filtering the index by market capitalization, this analysis stress-tests large-cap equities during an exogenous macro event. By peeling back the layers of the benchmark, we can expose the hidden rotational dynamics and severe risk fractures that occurred during the crash.


Section 1: The Benchmark vs. The Breadth

Masking the Rotational Damage Beneath the Surface
Purpose & Implications: The purpose of this analysis is to get a brief description of the stocks that outperformed, the sectors that they come from, and how it compares to a market-cap weighted perspective of this.
So the results were that Industrials, Financials, Information Technology, and Healthcare seem to have outperformed on initial glance on a numerical basis in terms of the number of companies. However, on a market-cap weighted basis, which the S&P500 index number would reflect, Information Technology seems to be much heavier weighted, which could lead to a skew of results.
An interesting direction could be exploring how many companies are in each sector that we have graphed here and so what percentage of each sector actually outperformed and by how much. But these graphs give an initial view into the data.

Section 2: Stress-Testing the Large-Cap Universe

The Outperformance Elimination Pipeline
Purpose & Implications: The purpose of this visualization is to map the sequential filtering of the Russell 1000 universe down to a concentrated pool of consistent large-cap outperformers.
So we know that we start with 1000 stocks thereabouts, so this sankey diagram helps to visualise the narrowing down of the stocks in stages, from the eligible large-caps, to those that outperformed period 1 to the stocks that outperformed period 2.

import plotly.graph_objects as go
import pandas as pd

# 2. Define node labels and indices (the order determines positions)
# Indices: 0-Start, 1-Excluded, 2-Eligible, 3-Won P1, 4-Lost P1, 
# 5-Consistent P1/P2, 6-P1 Winner/P2 Loser, 7-P2 Only Winner, 8-P1 Loser/P2 Loser
node_labels = [
    "Russell 1000 (Start)", 
    "Excluded (<$30B)", 
    "Eligible Large-Caps", 
    "Outperform P1", 
    "Underperform P1",

    "Outperform P2 Only (35)", # This preserves your total of 130 (95 + 35)
    "Underperform Both"
]

# (Simplified color definition for clarity, real chart uses gradients)
node_colors = ["#444", "#888", "#226B80", "#E0816C", "#666", "#019267", "#888", "#E0816C", "#444"]

# 3. Create the Sankey Diagram
fig = go.Figure(go.Sankey(
    orientation = "h", # Keep horizontal, it flows better
    node = dict(
        pad = 20, # More space between nodes
        thickness = 30,
        line = dict(color = "#eee", width = 0.5),
        label = node_labels,
        color = node_colors,
        # Positions: range [0,1] for x,y. You might need to refine these based on exact counts.

        y = [0.5, 0.1, 0.5, 0.25, 0.75, 0.10, 0.35, 0.60, 0.85],

    link = dict(
        source = [0, 0, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4, 4], # Indices of node_labels list
        target = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8],
        value = [excluded_count, eligible_count, p1_winners_count, p1_losers_count, consistent_count, 
                 p1_winners_count - consistent_count, p2_only_winners_count, p1_losers_count - p2_only_winners_count],

))

Section 3: The Efficiency Frontier

The Failure of Passive Beta in a Crisis
Purpose & Implications: This interactive Sortino matrix is designed to evaluate risk-adjusted performance across the universe of individual stocks. We do this analysis for the entire period just as an overview.
We plot Maximum Drawdown against the Sortino Ratio. This compares a portfolio’s worst-ever peak-to-trough decline during the period against the return per unit of bad volatility (risk-adjusted return). We use this to determine if the higher returns are worth the potential for bad losses, with a low maximum drawdown and a high Sortino ratio (>2) indicating superior, resilient performance.
The scatter plot reveals that if you held the S&P 500 in Q1 2026, you “won” on stability (low drawdown) but “lost” on efficiency (low Sortino). This demonstrates that “beating the market” in this period required picking specific winners in sectors like Industrials or Real Estate and having the stomach to ride out drawdowns that were 2x to 3x worse than the index itself.


Show Data Processing Code
# 1. Calculate S&P 500 Benchmark Metrics
risk_free_rate = 0.045 / 252
daily_ret_sp = sp500.pct_change().dropna()

# Benchmark Sharpe
excess_sp = daily_ret_sp - risk_free_rate
sp500_sharpe = np.sqrt(252) * (excess_sp.mean() / excess_sp.std())

# Benchmark Sortino
downside_sp = daily_ret_sp[daily_ret_sp < 0].std() * np.sqrt(252)
sp500_sortino = ((daily_ret_sp.mean() * 252) - 0.045) / downside_sp if downside_sp != 0 else np.nan

# Benchmark Max Drawdown
cum_ret_sp = (1 + daily_ret_sp).cumprod()
sp500_max_dd = ((cum_ret_sp - cum_ret_sp.cummax()) / cum_ret_sp.cummax()).min()

# 1. Create a dictionary to map Tickers to Full Company Names

    # Sortino
    downside_std = (daily_ret[daily_ret < 0]).std() * np.sqrt(252)
    sortino = ((daily_ret.mean() * 252) - 0.045) / downside_std if downside_std != 0 else np.nan
    
    # Max Drawdown

Section 4: Sector Deltas, Mean and Median changes from Period 1 to Period 2

Identifying Shock Absorbers and Value Traps
Purpose & Implications: The objective of this distribution plot is to evaluate the internal variance and shift in returns across different sectors.
The graph looks abit confusing and its sorted by the best performing sectors for the two periods, measured by the highest mean change moving from period 1 to period 2.
The transition between Phase 1 and Phase 2 characterizes a “relief rally” centered on Growth and Financials, rather than a broad-based market recovery.
Following the onset of the Iran conflict, the Information Technology sector saw its median return stabilize and its volatility (error bars) compress significantly, moving from a highly speculative range to a more defined positive trend.
In contrast, defensive sectors like Consumer Staples and Utilities saw their median returns collapse, while Energy’s “war premium” appeared to evaporate as returns normalized.
This suggests that once the geopolitical risk transitioned from an “unknown” to a “known” quantity, investors aggressively rotated out of hedges and back into high-beta tech and cyclical financials, effectively de-risking the event’s potential for long-term economic disruption.

Section 5: Active Return Deltas and Z-score shifts
For this table, we aim to see how much change in active return each sector has provided, showing the alpha that the sector has provided over the beta of the market. We then take the difference between the active return in period 1 and period 2 and color code the returns.
We also look at the z-score shift, which is the return for each sector normalised to the normal distribution of returns for the market. A higher numer shows a returns that performed outside of the standard deviation of returns as expected for stocks in the market and a lower z-score shows the reverse. So we also got the period 1 to period 2 change and color coded them, showing that IT had a large improvement in returns, when normalised and benchmarked to the rest of the market.

sp500_p2 = (1 + sp500_returns.loc[p2_start:p2_end]).prod() - 1

p2_sector_returns = (1 + sector_daily.loc[p2_start:p2_end]).prod() - 1

    'Phase 1 Return': p1_sector_returns,
    'Phase 2 Return': p2_sector_returns

# --- METHOD 1: Active Return Shift ---
sector_phase_returns['P1 Active Return'] = sector_phase_returns['Phase 1 Return'] - sp500_p1
sector_phase_returns['P2 Active Return'] = sector_phase_returns['Phase 2 Return'] - sp500_p2
sector_phase_returns['Active Return Delta'] = sector_phase_returns['P2 Active Return'] - sector_phase_returns['P1 Active Return']

# --- METHOD 2: Cross-Sectional Z-Scoring ---
p1_mean = sector_phase_returns['Phase 1 Return'].mean()
p1_std = sector_phase_returns['Phase 1 Return'].std()

p2_mean = sector_phase_returns['Phase 2 Return'].mean()
p2_std = sector_phase_returns['Phase 2 Return'].std()

sector_phase_returns['P1 Z-Score'] = (sector_phase_returns['Phase 1 Return'] - p1_mean) / p1_std
sector_phase_returns['P2 Z-Score'] = (sector_phase_returns['Phase 2 Return'] - p2_mean) / p2_std
sector_phase_returns['Z-Score Shift'] = sector_phase_returns['P2 Z-Score'] - sector_phase_returns['P1 Z-Score']

# Sort by the new Z-Score Shift to see who actually gained relative dominance
advanced_shift_df = sector_phase_returns.sort_values(by='Z-Score Shift', ascending=False)

Section 6: Sectorial Scatter Plot
For this plot, we revisit the scatter plot, but instead of individual companies we plot the sectors on the scatter plot. For this we use the numbers that we have derived above to get a better visualisation for that.

title: Iran War Impact Analysis: Regime Shifts & Market Volatility
section: Part 2: Algorithmic Regime Detection & Factor Modeling
text:
Part 2: Algorithmic Regime Detection & Factor Modeling
Applying Institutional Quantitative Models to Extract Idiosyncratic Alpha.
(The following sections are a work in progress unfortunately until Jordan can find more time to work on these :(((( ).

Section 1: Redefining the Market Driver (PCA)
Principal Component Analysis reveals a dramatic drop in PC1 (the “Market” factor) from 62% to 46% following the shock. This mathematically proves the transition from a macro-driven, high-correlation environment to a stock-picker’s market where idiosyncratic risk dominates.


Section 2: The Fama-MacBeth Regressions
Time-series analysis of Factor Premiums indicates exactly which fundamentals the market favored. The data proves the market aggressively bid up Cash Flow and violently punished high Accruals on February 27th, completely rewriting the standard factor hierarchy.


Section 3: Geographic & Supply Chain Risk (Compustat Segments)
By breaking down corporate revenue via Compustat segment data, we see that exposure to international revenue and supply chains was a far heavier anchor on performance than a company’s traditional sector classification.


Section 4: The Multi-Feature Hidden Markov Model (HMM)

Algorithmic Crisis Classification
Purpose & Implications: The purpose of this Hidden Markov Model is to programmatically detect unobservable market regimes using price volatility as emission probabilities. The algorithm successfully clusters the time-series into two distinct states: “Normal” and “Crisis.” The implication for quantitative trading is profound: instead of relying on lagging fundamental indicators, systemic models can autonomously trigger risk-off hedging protocols the moment the HMM transitions into a high-volatility state.



Section 5: Deconstructing the “Beta Trap” (Dynamic Correlation)

Exposing False Alpha via Rolling Covariance
Purpose & Implications: This rolling correlation chart measures the dynamic relationship between the “outperforming” Energy sector and the broader S&P 500 benchmark. The results show that Energy’s correlation spiked perfectly in tandem with the market’s pre-shock rally, indicating its gains were beta-driven rather than idiosyncratic alpha. The implication is that assets masquerading as alpha generators in a bull market will fail to provide diversification when the correlation matrix approaches \(1.0\) during a systemic shock.

title: Singapore Insurance Strategy Dashboard
text:
Act 1: The Deterministic Baseline
The first layer builds the standard financial projection.
- Liquidity Analysis: Comparing the Surrender Value of WL against the liquid portfolio of BTID.
- Legacy Visualization: Tracking the Death Benefit sum assured over time.
- The ‘Strategy Frontier’: A dynamic calculation that pinpoints the exact age where BTID overtakes WL based on user-defined investment returns (e.g., 4% vs 7%).

title: Singapore Insurance Strategy Dashboard
text:
Act 2: Behavioral Stress Testing
Data shows that most retail investors do not reinvest their savings perfectly. I introduced a “Discipline Coefficient”, a slider allowing users to see what happens if they only invest 50%, 70%, or 90% of their premium savings.

Key Insight: Through this feature, I discovered that if an investor’s discipline drops below ~60%, Whole Life plans often outperform BTID due to their “forced savings” nature, despite lower internal rates of return.

title: Singapore Insurance Strategy Dashboard
text:
Act 3: Stochastic Reality (Monte Carlo)
Life isn’t linear. Using Monte Carlo simulations, I modeled:
- Actuarial Risk: Probabilities of Death and Total Permanent Disability (TPD) using Singapore’s S0408 tables.
- Sequence of Returns Risk: Instead of a flat 7% return, the model simulates market volatility to show the range of possible outcomes for the BTID portfolio.

title: The God of Small Things
section: Summary and the Core Premise of the Book
text:
Summary and the Core Premise of the Book
A non-linear tragedy set in Kerala, exploring how the rigid, inescapable structures of caste, history, and social reputation (The “Big Things”) crush individual innocence, human connection, and vulnerable people (The “Small Things”).

title: The God of Small Things
text:
Why I Read This Book
Much like Steinbeck’s work, I am drawn to narratives that capture the lifelike, intricate details of side characters while examining how grand, arbitrary social structures ultimately dictate human behavior and family decay.

title: The God of Small Things
text:
Key Takeaways

The “Love Laws”: The novel exposes the violent enforcement of societal rules that dictate “who should be loved, and how. And how much.”
The Weaponization of Silence: Silence is not an absence of noise, but a structural tool used both as a shield by the powerless and a weapon by the powerful to maintain a fragile status quo.
Cultural Dislocation: An obsession with Anglophile, bourgeois culture deliberately blinds a family to the shifting socio-political reality of their immediate environment, accelerating their downfall.
Linguistic Subversion: The deconstruction of language through a child’s perspective serves to brilliantly satirize the adult world’s obsession with “correctness” and social norms over actual human connection.

title: The God of Small Things
text:
Reflection
This was a really good book, really enjoyed the writing style that Roy uses, as well as the themes in the book and especially the non-linear style of the book, watching it all unfold like a slow and unavoidable train wreck. Characters all feel really lifelike and have their own rich inner lives and motivations, with the characters coming together in realistic ways, each playing their part to allow the inevitable tragedy to unfold.

title: The God of Small Things
text:
Favorite Quotes


The Asymmetry of “Big” and “Small” Things
The novel argues that the “Big Things” (caste, politics, the police, and societal expectations) are vast, crushing machines that demand compliance. The “Small Things” (touch, personal intimacy, a child’s game) are deeply human but structurally powerless.
This dynamic is perfectly captured during the family’s tense drive from the airport, where “big things were not spoken, only small things.” To preserve social face (mianzi) and prevent the family unit from exploding over racial tension and deep-seated humiliations, they retreat into trivial chitchat. They use “small things” as a lie, a distraction to cover up the ugly truth. Conversely, for Velutha—the God of Small Things—the small things are the only truth. The tragedy lies in the fact that while the Big Things usually win the battle, the Small Things are the only elements that give life meaning.


Silence as an Institutional Weapon
Silence operates as a heavy, physical presence throughout the narrative. It manifests in various forms: the traditional Kathakali dancer silenced when his ancient art is condensed into a 20-minute palatable performance for tourists; Mammachi silently enduring domestic abuse; or Comrade Pillai deliberately withholding the truth from the police. Characters like Baby Kochamma use silence—sending Ammu away—to eliminate threats to their own comfort. In this ecosystem, silence is a mechanism of survival for some, but a tool of complicity and destruction for others.


Cultural Dislocation and the “History House”
The family is trapped in the “History House,” alienated from their own reality by a deep-seated Anglophilia. Their obsession with English culture—quoting Shakespeare and idolizing The Sound of Music—is an attempt to maintain a “civilized,” bourgeois facade. However, this perfectly conflates with the stark reality of their surroundings in Kerala, which is undergoing a communist revolution. Their desperate need to associate with the “Anglo God” blinds them to the poverty and unrest around them, putting them directly in the destructive path of history.


The Subversion of Language
Roy masterfully uses the twins’ perspective to dismantle language. By phonetically deconstructing words (“Locusts Stand I” for locus standi) and reading backwards, the children create an insider language that highlights their vulnerability. Furthermore, the capitalization of abstract concepts—noting that a car is full of “Thoughts and Things to Say”—gives physical weight to the unsaid. To a child, a forbidden thought occupies as much tangible space in a room as a chair. This brilliantly satirizes an adult society that values “correct” pronunciation and polite appearances over recognizing the emotional reality of its children.


The Anatomy of Bitterness
While some characters are victims of systemic forces, others highlight the sheer destructive capacity of personal bitterness. Baby Kochamma operates purely out of self-interest, poisoning every situation she touches. Having briefly experienced the “small things” in her youth with Father Mulligan and subsequently losing them, she refuses to seek new meaning. Instead, she resolves to destroy the small things in the lives of everyone else. It is a sharp reminder that a family is just as easily rotted from within by individual spite as it is crushed from the outside by society.
